feedback.text: While I support the goals of the Digital Fairness Act, I believe an additional provision should be included that specifically addresses video games that require a constant online connection to the publishers' servers. At present, publishers are not obligated to implement an end-of-life plan for these games. As a result, when support is discontinued and servers are taken offline, the games, often purchased in good faith by consumers (!!) become unplayable, potentially permanently. This is not a theoretical concern. It has already affected hundreds of titles. The widespread frustration with this practice is reflected in the Stop Destroying Video Games European Citizens' Initiative, which has gathered over 1.4 million signatures. Clearly, this is an issue that resonates with citizens across the EU. Video games are not just entertainment. They are cultural artifacts of the 21st century. Each title represents a unique creative work that cannot simply be substituted or recreated. Therefore, I strongly urge that the Digital Fairness Act include measures to prevent publishers from rendering purchased games unusable by discontinuing server support without offering viable alternatives.
